annotations.label: First fundamental theorem of calculus

anchor.snippet: Let f be a continuous real-valued function defined on a closed interval

annotations.note: States that for continuous f, u ↦ ∫ x in a..u, f x has strict derivative f b at b; see also Continuous.deriv_integral.

annotations.label: Corollary (evaluation via antiderivative)

anchor.snippet: The fundamental theorem is often employed to compute the definite integral

annotations.note: If deriv f = f' with f differentiable and f' continuous on [a, b], then ∫ y in a..b, f' y = f b - f a.

annotations.label: Second fundamental theorem of calculus (Newton–Leibniz)

anchor.snippet: This part is sometimes referred to as the second fundamental theorem of calculus

annotations.note: If f has derivative f' on [a,b] and f' is interval-integrable then ∫ y in a..b, f' y = f b - f a.

annotations.label: Generalization: Part I for Lebesgue integrable f continuous at a point

anchor.snippet: if f is any Lebesgue integrable function on

annotations.note: For an interval-integrable f continuous at b, u ↦ ∫ x in a..u, f x has strict derivative f b at b.

annotations.label: Generalization: Lebesgue differentiation theorem (higher dimensions)

anchor.snippet: In higher dimensions Lebesgue's differentiation theorem generalizes the Fundamental theorem of calculus

annotations.note: Locally integrable f satisfies the Lebesgue differentiation theorem at almost every point via the Vitali family framework (also Besicovitch.ae_tendsto_average_norm_sub).

annotations.label: Generalization: Part II for Lebesgue integrable functions with antiderivative

anchor.snippet: Part II of the theorem is true for any Lebesgue integrable function

annotations.note: Assumes only that f is continuous on [a,b], has derivative f' on (a,b), and f' is interval (Lebesgue/Bochner) integrable.

annotations.label: Generalization: absolutely continuous F and integrability of F'

anchor.snippet: However, if F is absolutely continuous

annotations.note: If f is absolutely continuous on uIcc a b, then ∫ x in a..b, deriv f x = f b - f a.

annotations.label: Generalization: Henstock–Kurzweil version

anchor.snippet: if a continuous function F ( x ) admits a derivative f ( x ) at all but countably many points

annotations.note: Mathlib lacks a one-dimensional Henstock–Kurzweil integral; the closest result is FTC2 for right-derivatives requiring f' to be Lebesgue-integrable, which is strictly weaker than the HK version.

annotations.label: Generalization: complex functions with holomorphic antiderivative

anchor.snippet: suppose U is an open set in C and f : U → C is a function that has a holomorphic antiderivative

annotations.note: Mathlib has curveIntegral and primitive existence (Complex.IsConservativeOn.isExactOn_ball, DifferentiableOn.isExactOn_ball) but no direct theorem evaluating a path integral as F(γ(1)) − F(γ(0)) when F is a holomorphic primitive.
